lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — Frontier: the post-checkpoint era
Let me close with a look forward.
Checkpoint inhibitors are the foundation.
What is being built on top of that foundation?
Where is the field going in the next ten or twenty years?
Bispecific checkpoint inhibitors.
Antibodies engineered to bind two targets at once — for example, both PD-1 and CTLA-4, or PD-1 and LAG-3.

The hope is that a single molecule targeting two checkpoints simultaneously can deliver combination efficacy with potentially better safety, by ensuring that brake-cutting only happens where both targets are present.
Several bispecifics are in late-stage development.
Cadonilimab, from Akeso, is a PD-1/CTLA-4 bispecific that has been approved in China and is being tested globally.
Others are coming.

A particular subtype, PD-1/VEGF bispecifics, like ivonescimab, has produced striking early data — head-to-head against pembrolizumab in lung cancer, ivonescimab improved progression-free survival meaningfully in a Chinese Phase 3.
Confirmatory global trials are ongoing.
If those results hold, ivonescimab could disrupt the entire first-line lung cancer market.
Cytokine-based therapy.
Before checkpoint inhibitors, the original cancer immunotherapies were cytokines — interleukin-2 (IL-2) and interferon-alpha.
They worked, occasionally, in melanoma and renal cell carcinoma.
They were also extraordinarily toxic — high-dose IL-2 was given in ICUs, with significant treatment-related mortality.
The toxicity made them unfeasible in most settings.
But the cytokine concept is being revived, with engineering.

Engineered IL-2 variants — molecules that preferentially activate effector T cells rather than regulatory T cells, that have longer half-lives, that have tumor-targeted delivery — are in active development.
IL-15 and IL-12, two other immune-activating cytokines, are being similarly engineered.
The hope is that the next-generation cytokines will have the efficacy of the originals without the toxicity.
Combinations with checkpoint inhibitors are an obvious starting point.
STING agonists.
STING — stimulator of interferon genes — is a pathway in immune cells that, when activated, triggers a type-I interferon response and drives T-cell infiltration into tumors.
Small molecules that activate STING are an attractive way to heat up cold tumors.
The clinical development has been bumpy — early agents had pharmacokinetic problems and disappointing trial results — but newer molecules and intratumoral delivery approaches are reopening the field.
TGF-beta blockade.
TGF-beta is one of the major immunosuppressive cytokines in the tumor microenvironment.
Drugs that block TGF-beta in combination with checkpoint inhibitors are being explored, with the rationale that lifting two layers of suppression simultaneously may work where one is not enough.
CAR-T cells for solid tumors.

CAR-T cells — engineered T cells expressing chimeric antigen receptors — have transformed treatment of certain blood cancers, with multiple FDA-approved products for leukemia, lymphoma, and multiple myeloma.
Extending CAR-T to solid tumors has been much harder, in part because solid tumors are immunosuppressive environments that exhaust the CAR-T cells quickly.
Combinations of CAR-T with checkpoint inhibitors — to keep the CAR-T cells fresh — are an obvious experimental direction.
TIL therapy.
Tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte therapy involves harvesting T cells from a patient's own tumor, expanding them outside the body, and reinfusing huge numbers of tumor-specific T cells.
Lifileucel was approved by the FDA in 2024 for advanced melanoma, the first TIL therapy approval.
It works in some patients who have failed checkpoint inhibitors, suggesting that — at least in melanoma — adoptive transfer can rescue some checkpoint-resistant disease.
If I had to bet on which of these directions will pay off biggest in the next decade, I'd put my money on three things.

First, mRNA neoantigen vaccines plus checkpoint inhibitors will become standard of care in multiple high-risk adjuvant settings, particularly melanoma and lung cancer, and possibly colorectal and pancreatic.

Second, bispecific antibodies targeting checkpoint plus angiogenesis — PD-1/VEGF combinations like ivonescimab — will displace some of the current chemo-plus-pembro regimens, particularly in lung cancer.
Third, TIL therapy and other adoptive cell approaches will become real options for checkpoint-refractory patients, though they will remain expensive and centralized.
And one more, the wild card: single-cell biology and AI-driven biomarker discovery will, over the next ten years, give us much better tools for predicting who responds to which therapy.
We will move from PD-L1 and TMB to integrated multi-omic biomarkers that capture immune state, tumor genotype, and microenvironment characteristics simultaneously.
Treatment selection will become substantially more personalized, and response rates will improve as we stop giving drugs to patients who can't benefit from them.
What we are not going to have, in the foreseeable future, is a single drug that cures all cancers.
There is no such drug.
There never will be.
Cancer is too heterogeneous, too cleverly evolved, too varied across tissues and patients to surrender to any single molecule.

What we will have is a richer and richer combinatorial toolkit — checkpoint inhibitors, vaccines, cytokines, CAR-Ts, targeted therapies, chemotherapy, radiation — and increasingly accurate ways of matching the right combination to the right patient.
The Allison-Honjo discoveries unlocked the room.
The next two decades will be about filling the room with furniture.
So let me close where we started.
Twenty-five years ago, James Allison made an antibody against a brake on T cells and watched mice cure themselves of tumors.
Thirty years ago, Tasuku Honjo cloned a gene from a dying T cell and spent the next decade figuring out it was a second brake.

Today, the drugs that came out of those discoveries are in the IV bags of millions of patients worldwide, and the cancers that used to kill in months now, for many of those patients, can be turned into chronic conditions or, in the best cases, into cures.
We are living, in cancer therapy, in the post-checkpoint-inhibitor era.
The brakes have been cut.
The immune army has been unleashed.

The questions that remain — who responds, how to extend the benefit, how to manage the toxicity, how to combine with everything else — are the questions that will define the next generation of oncology.
You are walking out of this lecture into a world where, increasingly, cancer is being treated by tuning your own immune system rather than poisoning your tumor.
That is a revolution.
It is one of the great achievements of modern biology.
And it is, even now, only getting started.
Thank you.
